runs.reasoningSummary: I think the central value is modestly positive and below one, reflecting common microsimulation practice of using a smaller intensive-margin response for secondary earners than for primary earners. My uncertainty is wide because implementations vary substantially across models and contexts.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation":"Secondary-earner substitution-response elasticity used in a U.S. microsimulation model, for secondary earners in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation.","point_estimate":0.35,"quantiles":{"p05":0.1,"p25":0.2,"p50":0.35,"p75":0.6,"p95":1},"citations":["Goldin (1980s labor supply / secondary earner responsiveness)","Eissa and Hoynes (1990s married women tax labor supply estimates)","Institute for Fiscal Studies-style tax-benefit microsimulation secondary earner elasticity defaults"],"reasoning_summary":"I think the central value is modestly positive and below one, reflecting common microsimulation practice of using a smaller intensive-margin response for secondary earners than for primary earners. My uncertainty is wide because implementations vary substantially across models and contexts."}
